questions.question: What is the fundamental criterion to define nephrotic syndrome in children?
questions.explanation: The main diagnostic criterion of nephrotic syndrome is massive proteinuria, defined as urinary protein excretion greater than 40 mg/m2/h in children, which leads to hypoalbuminemia and edema.

questions.question: What proteinuria value in 24 hours confirms infantile nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: In clinical practice, nephrotic proteinuria in children is defined as excretion greater than 50 mg/kg/day or 40 mg/m2/h, which is equivalent to a urinary protein/creatinine ratio > 2 mg/mg.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding is characteristic of nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Hypoalbuminemia, with serum albumin values ​​less than 2.5 g/dL, is a central finding in nephrotic syndrome due to massive loss of protein in the urine.

questions.question: What is the earliest complication of active nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Generalized edema is the earliest and most frequent clinical manifestation of nephrotic syndrome, secondary to hypoalbuminemia that reduces plasma oncotic pressure and favors sodium and water retention.

questions.question: What test confirms nephrotic proteinuria in children on an outpatient basis?
questions.explanation: The protein/creatinine ratio in an isolated urine sample is a practical and reliable method to estimate proteinuria; Values ​​> 2 mg/mg are indicative of nephrotic range in children.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a clinical criterion of nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Isolated glomerular hematuria is not part of the diagnostic criteria for classic nephrotic syndrome; The cardinal criteria are massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia and edema, with frequent hyperlipidemia.

questions.question: What is the most common age of presentation of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in children?
questions.explanation: Idiopathic nephrotic syndrome, mainly due to minimal changes, has its peak incidence between 2 and 6 years of age, being rare before one year of age.

questions.question: What histological type predominates in nephrotic syndrome with onset between 2 and 6 years?
questions.explanation: In children aged 2 to 6 years with nephrotic syndrome, minimal change disease represents approximately 80% of cases, being the most common cause.

questions.question: What is the typical initial clinical presentation of nephrotic syndrome in a 3-year-old child?
options: Macroscopic hematuria and low back pain
questions.explanation: Morning eyelid edema, which then generalizes, along with oliguria, is the classic presentation of nephrotic syndrome in preschoolers, associated with massive proteinuria.

questions.question: Which laboratory finding is characteristic in a 4-year-old child with de novo nephrotic syndrome?
options: Proteinuria in the nephrotic range and hypoalbuminemia
questions.explanation: Proteinuria in the nephrotic range (>40 mg/m²/hour or protein/creatinine ratio >2) and hypoalbuminemia (<2.5 g/dL) are the defining findings of nephrotic syndrome at this age.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for a 3-year-old child with idiopathic nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The initial treatment of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in preschool children is oral corticosteroids (prednisone) for 4-6 weeks, with a good response in the majority.

questions.question: What infectious complication is most common in children aged 2 to 6 years with active nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, usually due to Streptococcus pneumoniae, is the most common infectious complication in children with nephrotic syndrome due to hypogammaglobulinemia and ascites edema.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in children?
questions.explanation: Minimal change disease is the most common cause of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in pediatric age, representing approximately 80-90% of cases.

questions.question: What histological finding characterizes minimal change nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Electron microscopy reveals diffuse podocyte fusion in minimal change disease, whereas light microscopy is usually normal.

questions.question: Which of the following is a diagnostic criterion for nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The main criterion is massive proteinuria (>50 mg/kg/day or >40 mg/m2/hour), together with hypoalbuminemia and edema, which define nephrotic syndrome.

questions.question: What clinical feature suggests secondary nephrotic syndrome in children?
questions.explanation: Persistent arterial hypertension is more common in secondary forms or in glomerulopathies other than minimal changes, such as focal glomerulosclerosis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in children?
questions.explanation: Oral prednisone is the standard initial treatment for idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in pediatrics, achieving remission in most cases with minimal changes.

questions.question: What infectious complication is most feared in childhood nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is a serious and frequent complication in children with nephrotic syndrome, due to secondary immunodeficiency due to loss of immunoglobulins.

questions.question: What is the most common histological lesion in children with nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Minimal change disease is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in pediatric age, representing approximately 80% of cases.

questions.question: What ultrastructural finding characterizes minimal change disease?
questions.explanation: Fusion of podocytes (pedicels) is the classic ultrastructural finding in minimal change disease, visible only with electron microscopy.

questions.question: What is the second most common histologic cause of nephrotic syndrome in older children?
questions.explanation: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis is the second most common lesion in older children and adolescents with nephrotic syndrome, and is often corticosteroid-resistant.

questions.question: What histologic pattern is typically associated with congenital nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Finnish congenital nephrotic syndrome is characterized by tubular microcysts and interstitial fibrosis, with a distinctive histology that does not correspond to minimal changes.

questions.question: What immunological deposit is frequently seen in focal segmental glomerulosclerosis?
questions.explanation: In focal and segmental glomerulosclerosis, it is common to find granular deposits of IgM and C3 in the areas of sclerosis, although it is not pathognomonic.

questions.question: What light microscopy shows IgA nephropathy in children with nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: IgA nephropathy in children usually presents diffuse mesangial proliferation on light microscopy, with mesangial IgA deposits confirmed by immunofluorescence.

questions.question: What is the corticosteroid of choice in the initial treatment of childhood nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Prednisone or prednisolone is the first-line corticosteroid for the initial therapy of idiopathic nephrotic syndrome in pediatrics, demonstrating efficacy in inducing remission.

questions.question: What is the usual daily dose of prednisone at the beginning of treatment for pediatric nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The recommended initial dose of prednisone is 2 mg/kg/day (maximum 60 mg/day) for 4-6 weeks, followed by alternating doses to induce remission in children with nephrotic syndrome.

questions.question: What term defines the expected response to initial corticosteroid treatment in childhood nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Complete remission, defined as negative or trace proteinuria for 3 consecutive days, is the expected response after 4-6 weeks of initial corticosteroid therapy in most children.

questions.question: What is the typical total duration of the first course of corticosteroids in infantile nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The initial course of prednisone usually lasts 12 weeks: 4-6 weeks at full daily dosing followed by 6-8 weeks at alternating dosing, according to standard pediatric protocols.

questions.question: What approximate percentage of children with nephrotic syndrome responds to initial treatment with corticosteroids?
questions.explanation: Approximately 90% of children with idiopathic nephrotic syndrome respond to corticosteroids, achieving complete remission with initial prednisone therapy.

questions.question: What is the main adverse effect that requires close monitoring during initial corticosteroid therapy in children?
questions.explanation: High blood pressure is a frequent and relevant adverse effect of corticosteroids in pediatrics, requiring regular monitoring of blood pressure during the treatment of nephrotic syndrome.

questions.question: What is the expected initial response in a child with corticoresistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Corticoresistance is defined as the persistence of proteinuria after 4 weeks of treatment with prednisone at full doses, indicating lack of response to corticosteroids.

questions.question: What classification is used to define the response to corticosteroids in pediatric nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The response to corticosteroids is classified as complete (remission) or partial (reduction in proteinuria without normalizing), being the basis for deciding additional immunosuppressive treatment.

questions.question: What is the first-line drug in corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: In corticosteroid resistance, cyclosporine is the first-line treatment, since it induces remission in a significant percentage of patients, although it requires monitoring of kidney levels and function.

questions.question: What urinary finding defines complete remission in pediatric nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Complete remission is defined as negative proteinuria or traces on a dipstick for at least 3 consecutive days, indicating disappearance of nephrotic proteinuria.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome in children?
questions.explanation: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is the most common histological lesion in children with corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome, requiring renal biopsy for confirmation.

questions.question: What duration of treatment with corticosteroids defines corticosteroid dependence in pediatrics?
questions.explanation: Corticosteroid dependence is defined as two consecutive relapses during corticosteroid dose reduction or within 14 days after discontinuation.

questions.question: What is an absolute indication for renal biopsy in infantile nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Age less than 1 year is an absolute indication for renal biopsy in infantile nephrotic syndrome due to the high probability of non-minimal change etiology, such as focal and segmental glomerulonephritis or renal dysplasia.

questions.question: What clinical finding requires renal biopsy in pediatric nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Persistent microscopic hematuria is an indication for renal biopsy because it suggests proliferative glomerulonephritis or IgA nephropathy, etiologies that do not typically respond to corticosteroids and require histological diagnosis.

questions.question: When is kidney biopsy indicated in a child with corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: In corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome, renal biopsy is indicated after 8 weeks of treatment with corticosteroids without response, to rule out focal and segmental glomerulonephritis or other glomerulopathies that require specific immunosuppression.

questions.question: Which feature of congenital nephrotic syndrome indicates immediate renal biopsy?
questions.explanation: The onset of nephrotic syndrome in the first 3 months of life is considered congenital and always requires renal biopsy to rule out genetic mutations such as nephrin or podocin, which have a poor prognosis and do not respond to corticosteroids.

questions.question: What laboratory finding is an indication for renal biopsy in infantile nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Elevated creatinine indicates deterioration of renal function, which suggests advanced glomerular disease such as sclerosing glomerulonephritis, requiring a biopsy to define etiology and prognosis before instituting immunosuppressive treatment.

questions.question: What indication for renal biopsy is related to the response to treatment in nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Frequent relapses (more than 2 in 6 months or 4 in 12 months) are an indication for renal biopsy because they may be due to focal and segmental glomerulonephritis or IgA nephropathy, which require biopsy to adjust immunosuppressive therapy.

questions.question: What is the most common infection in children with nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis is the most common infection in children with nephrotic syndrome, due to immunodeficiency secondary to the loss of immunoglobulins and complement factors due to proteinuria.

questions.question: What microorganism is the most common cause of peritonitis in nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common agent in spontaneous bacterial peritonitis associated with nephrotic syndrome, followed by gram-negative bacilli such as Escherichia coli.

questions.question: What factor predisposes to thrombosis in nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The urinary loss of antithrombin III, together with other endogenous anticoagulants, generates a state of hypercoagulability that predisposes to venous thrombosis, especially in the renal vein.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic thrombosis in children with nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Renal vein thrombosis is the most characteristic in childhood nephrotic syndrome, due to urinary loss of anticoagulants and hemoconcentration associated with severe hypoalbuminemia.

questions.question: What prophylaxis is recommended for infections in nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Prophylaxis with daily oral penicillin is recommended in children with nephrotic syndrome to prevent Streptococcus pneumoniae infections, especially during outbreaks or in patients with hypogammaglobulinemia.

questions.question: What is the anticoagulant of choice to treat thrombosis in nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Subcutaneous enoxaparin is the initial anticoagulant of choice in children with thrombosis associated with nephrotic syndrome, due to its safety profile and predictable anti-Xa monitoring.
hashtags: Nelson Treatise of Pediatrics 21ed---ISTTH Pediatric Thrombosis Guide

questions.question: What defines a relapse in childhood nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Relapse is defined by proteinuria in 12-hour urine or protein/creatinine ratio >2 mg/mg, equivalent to >40 mg/m2/hour, for three consecutive days after achieving remission.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in a relapse of corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: In corticosteroid resistance, intravenous pulse methylprednisolone is the first-line treatment to try to induce remission, before resorting to second-line immunosuppressants such as cyclosporine or rituximab.

questions.question: What is the main poor prognostic factor in corticosteroid resistance of nephrotic syndrome?
options: Age of onset greater than 6 years
questions.explanation: Early progressive renal failure is the main poor prognostic factor in corticosteroid resistance, indicating minimal change disease with focal segmental sclerosis or rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of massive proteinuria in nephrotic syndrome?
options: Loss of negative charges from the basement membrane
questions.explanation: The loss of negative charges from the glomerular basement membrane, due to fusion of podocytes and alteration of proteoglycans, allows massive filtration of albumin, causing nephrotic proteinuria.

questions.question: When is corticosteroid resistance considered in a child with nephrotic syndrome?
options: No remission after 4 weeks of prednisone
Failure to respond to methylprednisolone pulses
Persistent proteinuria after 8 weeks of prednisone
Relapse within the first month of treatment
questions.explanation: Corticoresistance is defined as the absence of complete remission (negative proteinuria for 3 consecutive days) after 8 weeks of treatment with full-dose prednisone, eventually including intravenous pulses.

questions.question: What is the immunosuppressant of choice in frequent relapses of corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Cyclophosphamide is the immunosuppressant of choice in frequent relapses with corticosteroid resistance, administered orally for 8-12 weeks, achieving sustained remission and reducing the number of annual relapses.

questions.question: What is the first-line immunosuppressant in corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Cyclosporine A is considered the first-line immunosuppressant in steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome, according to pediatric guidelines, due to its effectiveness in inducing remission by inhibiting calcineurin and reducing proteinuria.

questions.question: What mechanism of action does cyclosporine A have in nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Cyclosporin A binds to cyclophilin forming a complex that inhibits calcineurin, preventing the dephosphorylation of nuclear factor of activated T cells (NF-AT) and reducing the production of proinflammatory cytokines.

questions.question: What is the main adverse effect of cyclosporine A that limits its long-term use?
questions.explanation: Chronic nephrotoxicity is the most limiting adverse effect of cyclosporine A, and may cause irreversible tubulointerstitial fibrosis, which is why close monitoring of renal function and serum levels of the drug is required.

questions.question: What biological drug is used as rescue in corticosteroid resistance after failure of calcineurins?
questions.explanation: Rituximab, an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody, is used as rescue therapy in corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome that does not respond to calcineurin inhibitors, achieving remission by depleting B lymphocytes and modulating the immune response.

questions.question: What parameter is monitored to adjust the dose of cyclosporine A in children?
questions.explanation: Serum trough levels of cyclosporine A are monitored to adjust the dose and maintain therapeutic concentrations between 100-200 ng/mL, minimizing the risk of nephrotoxicity and ensuring immunosuppressive efficacy.

questions.question: What is the usual treatment time with cyclosporine A before considering therapeutic failure?
questions.explanation: Therapeutic failure is considered if partial or complete remission is not achieved after 4 to 6 months of treatment with cyclosporine A at adequate doses, at which time a change to another immunosuppressive therapy such as rituximab is evaluated.

questions.question: What is the most common genetic cause of congenital nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Mutation in the NPHS1 gene, which encodes nephrin, is the most common cause of congenital nephrotic syndrome Finnish type, an autosomal recessive form.

questions.question: Which glomerular filtration diaphragm protein is encoded by NPHS1?
questions.explanation: NPHS1 encodes nephrin, a key protein of the filtration diaphragm that binds podocyte pedicels; Its mutation causes congenital nephrotic syndrome.

questions.question: What is the inheritance pattern of NPHS1 congenital nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Congenital nephrotic syndrome Finnish type due to mutation in NPHS1 is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner, requiring two mutated alleles for expression.

questions.question: What clinically characterizes Finnish type congenital nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: It is characterized by massive proteinuria detected at birth or in the first three months, with generalized edema and ascites, due to alteration of the filtration diaphragm.

questions.question: What first-line treatment is used in corticosteroid-resistant congenital nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Genetic congenital nephrotic syndrome is usually corticosteroid-resistant; Kidney transplant is the definitive treatment, since it corrects the structural defect of the native kidney.

questions.question: Which gene is associated with childhood-onset nephrotic syndrome and diffuse mesangial sclerosis?
questions.explanation: Mutations in WT1 (tumor suppressor gene) cause nephrotic syndrome with diffuse mesangial sclerosis and association with Wilms tumors, manifesting in early childhood.

questions.question: What is the main prognostic factor in childhood nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The response to corticosteroids is the main prognostic factor in childhood nephrotic syndrome, since corticosteroid-resistant patients have worse long-term renal evolution.

questions.question: What long-term complication is most common in children with corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Children with corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome have a high risk of progression to chronic kidney disease, requiring prolonged nephrological follow-up.

questions.question: What is the expected frequency of relapses in corticoresistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Corticoresistant nephrotic syndrome usually presents frequent relapses, with more than three episodes per year, which worsens the renal prognosis.

questions.question: What laboratory parameter is used to evaluate remission in the follow-up of nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: The protein/creatinine ratio in morning urine is the standard parameter for evaluating remission, since it reflects proteinuria accurately and non-invasively.

questions.question: What is the relapse rate at 5 years in children with steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: More than 80% of children with steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome present relapses at 5 years, which justifies close nephrological follow-up.

questions.question: What intervention reduces the risk of progression to chronic kidney disease in corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome?
questions.explanation: Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors reduce proteinuria and slow the progression to chronic kidney disease in patients with corticosteroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome.
